section.title: Overview
section.para: The rust module is an &osips; module written entirely in Rust. It provides compiled-in &sip; processing functions (rate limiting, routing cache, &http; queries, atomic counters) alongside a runtime scripting engine (rust_exec()) that loads user-supplied handlers from shared libraries at startup.

The module supports both synchronous and asynchronous execution. When rust_exec() is called inside an async() block, handlers can perform blocking I/O without tying up &sip; worker processes — the same pattern used by the rest_client module.
Key features:

listitem.para: Per-caller rate limiting — Thread-local counters that survive across &sip; transactions without locking or shared state corruption.
In-memory routing cache — TTL-based destination cache using thread-local storage, providing lock-free O(1) lookups.
Persistent &http; connection pool — Maintains TCP connections to external &http; services across requests.
Cross-worker atomic counter — Shared-memory atomic counter visible to all worker processes.
Runtime scripting — Load user-written Rust handlers at startup via dlopen, callable from routes via rust_exec().
Async I/O — Handlers can perform blocking I/O inside async() blocks without tying up &sip; worker processes.
— Two-crate workspace, C shim strategy, sip_msg opacity, panic safety, version probing.
— Docker, bare metal, &osips; Make system integration, deployment and verification.
— Writing rust_exec() handlers with opensips_handler!, PV/AVP access, async patterns, and a reference for all 20 included handlers.

section.para: The source tree also includes example &osips; configurations in examples/: a basic config using compiled-in functions, an async &http; query config with resume routes, a scripting config demonstrating rust_exec(), and a reference user script with 19 handlers.

section.title: &osips; Modules
section.para:
The following modules must be loaded before this module:
signaling — Required by rust_check_rate() for sending 429 replies.
sl — Stateless reply support.

section.title: External Libraries or Applications
section.para:
The following libraries or applications must be installed before building the module:
Rust toolchain (1.70 or higher, install via rustup)
clang, llvm-dev, libclang-dev (for bindgen FFI generation)
pkg-config, libssl-dev (for reqwest &http; client)
All Rust dependencies are statically linked into the .so — no runtime library dependencies beyond what &osips; itself requires.

section.title: max_rate (integer)
section.para: Maximum number of requests allowed per source &ip; within the rate limiting window. When exceeded, returns -1 and sends a 429 Too Many Requests reply automatically.
Each worker maintains its own counter (thread-local). The effective global rate limit is approximately max_rate * udp_workers.

section.title: window_seconds (integer)
section.para: Duration of the rate limiting window in seconds. Counters reset at the start of each new window. Uses monotonic time — immune to NTP adjustments.

section.title: cache_ttl (integer)
section.para: Time-to-live for routing cache entries in seconds. Expired entries are lazily evicted on the next lookup. Each worker has an independent cache (thread-local, no sharing between workers).

section.title: http_timeout (integer)
section.para: Timeout for &http; requests in seconds. Applies to connection establishment, TLS handshake, and response transfer. Used by both and user-script &http; handlers.

section.title: pool_size (integer)
section.para: Maximum number of idle &http; connections to keep per host in the connection pool. Initialized during child_init for each worker process.

section.title: script_name (string)
section.para: Absolute path to a user-supplied shared library (.so) containing handler functions for . The library is loaded via dlopen(RTLD_NOW | RTLD_LOCAL) during mod_init.
When not set, returns -1 with an error log. All other compiled-in functions operate normally regardless of this parameter.

para.emphasis: Default value is not set (no user script).

section.title: Exported Functions

section.para: On failure (rate exceeded), a 429 Too Many Requests reply is sent automatically via sl_send_reply. The caller should exit after a -1 return.
Parameters: none

listitem.para: 1 - Request is within the rate limit (allow).
-1 - Rate limit exceeded. A 429 reply has been sent. Also returned if source &ip; extraction fails (logged at ERR level).

section.para: This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE and FAILURE_ROUTE.
Look up the R-URI user ($rU) in the per-worker routing cache. On a cache hit, sets $du (destination &uri;) to the cached value and returns 1.
Parameters: none

listitem.para: 1 - Cache hit. $du has been set to the cached destination.
-1 - Cache miss, expired entry, or empty $rU.
$rU is empty or not set — returns -1, logged at DBG level.
set_dst_uri fails — returns -1, logged at ERR level.

section.para: This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE.

section.section:
rust_cache_store()
Store the current R-URI user ($rU) and destination &uri; ($du) in the per-worker routing cache. The entry expires after seconds.
Parameters: none &rust_return_codes;
Error Conditions
$rU is empty or not set — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
$du is empty or not set — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE.
rust_cache_store usage
... $du = "sip:backend.example.com"; rust_cache_store(); ...

section.para: Perform a blocking &http; GET request using the persistent connection pool. The response body is stored in $var(http_result) and the &http; status code in $var(http_status).
Warning: This function blocks the &sip; worker for the duration of the &http; call (up to seconds). For non-blocking &http;, use with an async handler.
Parameters:

listitem.para: url (string) - Target &url;. Supports variable expansion.
$var(http_result) (string) - &http; response body. Empty on failure.
$var(http_status) (integer) - &http; status code (200, 404, etc.). 0 on failure.
-1 - Request failed (connection error, timeout, DNS failure, empty &url;, pool not initialized).
Empty or null &url; — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
DNS resolution failure — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
Connection refused — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
TLS handshake failure — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
&http; pool not initialized (child_init failed) — returns -1, logged at ERR level.

section.para: This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE.

example.title: rust_http_query usage

section.para: Increment the cross-worker atomic counter and store the new value in $var(shared_count). The counter is allocated in shared memory during mod_init (before fork) and uses hardware atomic instructions across all workers.
Parameters: none

listitem.para: $var(shared_count) (integer) - Counter value after increment.
1 - Always succeeds. Atomic operations cannot fail.

section.para: This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE and ONREPLY_ROUTE.

section.section:
rust_exec(function [, param])
Call a function exported by the user script loaded via . The function must be declared with opensips_handler! in the user crate.
Parameters:
function (string) - Name of the exported function in the user script.
param (string, optional) - Argument passed to the handler. Supports variable expansion.
&rust_return_codes;
Error Conditions
not configured — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
Function not found in script (dlsym fails) — returns -1, logged at ERR level with the symbol name.
Empty function name — returns -1, logged at ERR level.
Handler panic (Rust unwind caught at FFI boundary) — returns -1, logged at ERR level with the panic message.
This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE, FAILURE_ROUTE, and ONREPLY_ROUTE.
rust_exec usage
... # Call with no parameter rust_exec("hello_world");
# Call with a parameter rust_exec("greet", "howdy");
# Routing decision based on return value if (!rust_exec("check_caller")) { sl_send_reply(403, "Forbidden"); exit; }
# Variable expansion in parameter rust_exec("lookup", "$rU");
# Variable exchange: set input, call handler, read output $var(input) = "uppercase:hello world"; rust_exec("variable_exchange"); xlog("L_INFO", "Result: $var(output)\n"); ...

section.title: Exported Asynchronous Functions
section.section:
rust_exec(function [, param])
Perform an asynchronous call to a user script handler. This function behaves the same as in terms of input and processing, but in a non-blocking manner. Script execution is suspended until the handler's I/O operation completes.
When called inside async(), the module resolves an async-capable handler by looking for a symbol prefixed with async_ in the user script. For example, rust_exec("http_query") inside async() resolves async_http_query.
Async Handler Resolution
If async_{function} is found in the script — the async handler runs. It sets up non-blocking I/O and returns immediately. The reactor monitors the fd and invokes the resume callback when data arrives.
If async_{function} is not found — the sync handler runs as a fallback. The resume route executes immediately after completion. All existing handlers work inside async() without modification.
If neither variant is found — returns -1.
Resume Route
The resume route receives:
$rc — the handler's return code.
All pseudo-variables written by the handler (e.g., $var(http_status)).
All AVPs written by the handler (e.g., $avp(http_hdr)).
&rust_return_codes;
Additional Error Conditions (Async)
No async variant found — falls back to synchronous execution, logged at DBG level.
Async handler panic — returns -1, resume route may not execute. Logged at ERR level with the panic message.
Async fd monitoring timeout — the operation is cancelled. Timeout is set by the handler (default: no timeout unless the handler calls ctx.set_timeout()).
Resume callback panic — returns ASYNC_DONE, pseudo-variables may be incomplete. Logged at ERR level.
This function can be used from REQUEST_ROUTE.
async rust_exec usage
... route { # Non-blocking HTTP query async(rust_exec("http_query", "http://api.internal:8080/route/$rU"), api_done); }
route[api_done] { if ($var(http_status) == 0) { xlog("L_ERR", "HTTP error: $var(http_error)\n"); sl_send_reply(503, "Service Unavailable"); exit; }
xlog("L_INFO", "HTTP $var(http_status) in $var(http_time_ms)ms\n");
# Access JSON response xlog("L_DBG", "JSON: $var(http_json)\n");
# Iterate response headers (stacked AVPs) while ($avp(http_hdr)) { xlog("L_DBG", " $avp(http_hdr)\n"); $avp(http_hdr) = NULL; }
if ($var(http_status) == 200) { $du = $var(http_body); t_relay(); exit; }
sl_send_reply(404, "Not Found"); } ...
async_http_query Output Variables
The reference async handler (async_http_query, handler 20) sets the following variables in the resume route:
Pseudo-Variables
$var(http_status) (integer) - &http; status code (200, 404, etc.). 0 on error.
$var(http_body) (string) - Response body, truncated to 3800 bytes.
$var(http_json) (string) - Full response as JSON: {"status":200,"url":"...","elapsed_ms":42,"headers":{...},"body":"..."}
$var(http_error) (string) - Error message. Empty on success.
$var(http_time_ms) (integer) - Total elapsed time in milliseconds.
AVPs
$avp(http_hdr) (string, stacked) - Response headers, one per AVP entry. Iterate with while($avp(http_hdr)) and pop with $avp(http_hdr) = NULL.
async_http_query Error Conditions
No &url; provided — $var(http_status) = 0, $var(http_error) = no URL: set param or $var(http_url). Completes synchronously (ASYNC_SYNC); resume route still runs.
Non-http &url; scheme — $var(http_status) = 0, $var(http_error) = only http:// URLs supported. Completes synchronously.
Invalid port in &url; — $var(http_status) = 0, $var(http_error) = bad port in URL. Completes synchronously.
DNS resolution or connection failure — $var(http_status) = 0, $var(http_error) contains the system error. Completes synchronously.
Write failure (request could not be sent) — $var(http_status) = 0, $var(http_error) = write failed: .... Completes synchronously.
Response read failure (in resume callback) — $var(http_status) = 0, $var(http_error) = failed to read response. Resume route runs with empty data.
Response timeout (fd not readable within timeout period) — reactor cancels fd monitoring. Resume route may not run.

section.title: Thread-Local State
section.para: The rate limiter and routing cache use thread_local! storage. In &osips;'s pre-fork worker model, each child process gets its own copy after fork():

listitem.para: No locking, no mutexes, no data races.
Each worker's rate counter is independent (effective global rate = max_rate * udp_workers).
Each worker has its own cache (no sharing between workers).

section.title: Shared Memory
section.para: The cross-worker atomic counter allocates an AtomicI64 in &osips; shared memory during mod_init (before fork). The fetch_add instruction is truly atomic — no read-modify-write race.

section.title: Panic Safety
section.para: Every Rust function at the FFI boundary is wrapped with catch_unwind. If a panic occurs, it is caught and converted to a -1 return code. &osips; continues running. The panic message is logged at ERR level with the function name.
Async resume callbacks are also panic-safe — a panic returns ASYNC_DONE to prevent the reactor from re-calling a broken handler.

section.title: Symbol Isolation
section.para: User scripts are loaded with RTLD_LOCAL — their symbols do not leak into the global namespace. RTLD_NOW ensures all symbols are resolved at load time, failing fast at startup rather than at runtime.

section.title: Async Memory Model
section.para: For async() operations, the resume callback state is allocated on the Rust heap via Box::into_raw(). Since async() resumes in the same worker process, regular heap memory survives the reactor loop. The resume callback recovers ownership via Box::from_raw().
For async_launch() (fire-and-forget, different process may resume), shared memory (shm_malloc) would be required. The current implementation targets async() only.

section.title: Version Matching
section.para: The module extracts version strings from the &osips; source tree at compile time. Loading the module into a different &osips; version produces a module version mismatch error at startup. Always rebuild when upgrading &osips;.

section.title: Example Configurations
section.para: The source tree includes three example configurations in modules/rust/examples/. Each demonstrates a different set of module capabilities.

section.title: basic.cfg — Compiled-in Functions
section.para: Minimal config demonstrating the compiled-in functions: rate limiting, routing cache, and HTTP query.

section.title: async.cfg — Non-blocking HTTP
section.para: Non-blocking HTTP queries using async() with rust_exec(). The worker is released during the HTTP call and resumes when the response arrives.

section.title: scripting.cfg — User Script Handlers
section.para: Demonstrates rust_exec() with user-supplied handler scripts, exercising both sync handlers and async handlers in a single config.
